items.title: Montreal’s big night: Victoire look to clinch, Canadiens eye 3-1 series lead
items.summary_auto: It’s a busy night for Montreal sports fans as both of the city’s professional hockey teams prepare for high-stakes playoff matchups on home ice.

items.title: Memphis Grizzlies forward Brandon Clarke dead at 29
items.summary_auto: Brandon Clarke, a forward for the Memphis Grizzlies, has died at the age of 29.

items.title: World Cup will be missing some star players as injuries mount before big kickoff
items.summary_auto: Injuries to some of soccer's biggest names are mounting ahead of the World Cup which starts next month, including Kylian Mbappe, Lamine Yamal and Mohamed Salah.

items.title: Toronto is 'only city' that can host new international defence bank: Ford
items.summary_auto: Citing the city’s high concentration of bank headquarters and financial services, Ontario leaders are arguing that Toronto’s the only viable choice for the new multinational Defence, Security and Resilience Bank.

items.title: Cleaning out the shed or cottage for spring? Here’s how to protect yourself from hantavirus
items.summary_auto: As Canadians embark on spring cleaning, public health officials are advising them to take steps to protect themselves from hantavirus carried by rats and mice.

items.title: 'Raining down ash': Fast-moving wildfire continues to rage near Whitecourt, Alta.
items.summary_auto: A wildfire threatening homes near Whitecourt continues to rage a few kilometres southeast of the northern Alberta town.

items.title: Dunkin' coming back to Canada, following deal with Foodtastic
items.summary_auto: In a country dominated by Tim Hortons, will anyone run to Dunkin’? Montreal-based restaurant operator Foodtastic thinks so, and plans to bring hundreds of the doughnut shops to Canada as early as this year.

items.title: Liberal MP challenges nomination loss for Toronto-area byelection
items.summary_auto: Liberal MP Nate Erskine-Smith has filed a notice of appeal to the provincial Liberals, challenging the result of a nomination race that he lost.

items.title: U.S. indicts 2 companies, ship employee for deadly 2024 Baltimore bridge collapse
items.summary_auto: The U.S. Justice Department said on Tuesday a grand jury indicted two foreign operators and a shoreside superintendent in the March 2024 collision of the 984-foot cargo ‌ship Dali that destroyed Baltimore’s Francis Scott Key Bridge and killed six construction workers.

items.title: Suckerfish are diving headfirst into unsuspecting manta rays' rear-ends
items.summary_auto: Suckerfish, it turns out, are not always the most considerate travel companions to their manta ray hosts.

items.title: Ebay rejects $56 billion US bid by GameStop, calling it 'neither credible nor attractive'
items.summary_auto: GameStop CEO Ryan Cohen has argued that by combining GameStop and eBay, he could cut costs and find synergies to create a much bigger enterprise. But analysts and investors had wondered whether the offer by GameStop, which is much smaller than the company its trying to buy, stood a chance.

items.title: What to know ahead of the FIFA World Cup
items.summary_auto: The 2026 FIFA World Cup is coming to Canada — and it's going to be the biggest in the competition's history. It's the first to be hosted in three countries — and Canada is hoping it'll also have its first-ever World Cup win.

items.title: Alberta's chief electoral officer wants pause on sharing electors list until law amended
items.summary_auto: The province’s chief electoral officer says the province’s list of electors shouldn’t be shared with any political parties until the legislature makes changes to better protect Albertans’ personal information.

items.title: Canadian Armed Forces to put away the big guns, end avalanche management in B.C.'s Rogers Pass
items.summary_auto: For more than 70 years, the Canadian Armed Forces have managed the avalanche control in Glacier National Park, alongside Parks Canada. That unique partnership is set to end in August 2027, when the Memorandum of Understanding expires.

items.title: Should Canadian airports be privatized?
items.summary_auto: The federal government is mulling the privatization of Canadian airports. What are the true costs of a potential windfall?

items.title: Do a tick check every time you spend time outside, Guelph and area health unit says
items.summary_auto: Wellington-Dufferin-Guelph Public Health has updated their tick guidance as the population of arachnids grows locally and they're found in more locations, including in people's gardens.

items.title: Elliot Page gave $25K for a community well in N.S. It's still not running and he's not happy
items.summary_auto: Nova Scotia-born actor Elliot Page donated $25,000 for a well for people who live near Shelburne’s old garbage dump and fear contaminated water and higher cancer rates. The project has been fraught with delays, disagreement and division.

items.title: 'It has been devastating,' U.S. spirits group says about Canadian alcohol boycotts
items.summary_auto: Between some provinces not selling American alcohol — part of the response to the trade war started by the U.S. — and consumers choosing not to buy it, the actions have been "devastating," says the CEO of the Distilled Spirits Council of the United States.

items.title: British PM Starmer tells cabinet meeting he intends to remain leader despite calls to resign
items.summary_auto: British Prime Minister Keir Starmer defied calls to resign on Tuesday, telling ministers he would "get on with governing" despite a "destabilizing" 48 hours of growing calls to set out ​a timetable for his departure after an election drubbing.

items.title: Search continues for missing pair in Thunder Bay, Ont., last seen at abandoned grain elevators
items.summary_auto: Ashlynn Bottle, 23, and Nodin Skunk, 25, were last seen together at the old Pool 8 grain elevator in the 400 block of Syndicate Avenue South on April 26 in Thunder Bay, Ont., on the city’s south side. Here's what we know about ongoing efforts to locate the members of Mishkeegogamang First Nation.

items.title: Boys are falling behind in school, and some experts say it starts in kindergarten
items.summary_auto: A new report submitted to the Quebec government says boys are "systemically disadvantaged" in their school system. Meanwhile, new Health Canada data shows higher rates of young men aged 15 to 24 suffer from mental health issues.

items.title: Resilient Ottawa Charge looking for Walter Cup redemption
items.summary_auto: The Ottawa Charge learned valuable lessons in last year's Walter Cup final loss to the Minnesota Frost.

items.title: Heated Rivalry is getting a musical parody the director calls a 'love letter' to hit Canadian series
items.summary_auto: A fast-tracked musical parody that transforms Heated Rivalry, the queer Canadian hockey romance, into an off-Broadway production complete with a "wine-mom" narrator and campy showtunes that creators hope will feed a fandom hungry for more.

items.title: What Trump and Xi are looking to get out of this week's summit in Beijing
items.summary_auto: As U.S. President Donald Trump travels to Beijing to meet with Chinese President Xi Jinping, the expectations around the highly anticipated summit between the leaders of the world’s two largest economies are being tempered by the war against Iran, and as China appears to hold some key leverage.

items.title: Why Trump's plan to drop gas tax may leave U.S. drivers feeling empty
items.summary_auto: U.S. President Donald Trump is finally acknowledging the financial pain that Americans are feeling with the soaring cost of gas, but his plan to pause the federal fuel tax may not have the kind of impact either he or drivers hope for.

items.title: The cruise ship hantavirus outbreak offered a real-life simulation exercise. Did we pass?
items.summary_auto: Hantavirus took hold on a weeks-long cruise before global health officials had any idea that a rodent-borne illness was silently spreading. More people keep falling ill back on land. Scientists expect the outbreak will fizzle out, but the crisis has offered a reminder that viruses can spread faster than public health efforts.

items.title: Hantavirus doesn't have 'pandemic potential,' says health official. Why those fears remain
items.summary_auto: Public health officials have been clear that the hantavirus outbreak is not another COVID-19. But despite reassurances from experts in Canada and abroad that the vuris is not easily transmissible between humans, news of the passengers exposed on the MV Hondius cruise ship has triggered memories of the COVID-19 pandemic and fears of mass infections and lockdowns.

items.title: Trans rights group says some U.S. students denied care at McGill clinic due to Trump executive order
items.summary_auto: A group that advocates for trans health care says McGill University’s wellness hub is refusing to provide hormone replacement therapy to U.S. students under the age of 19 because of fears of repercussions from the Trump administration.
